lesson:
Japanese has three words for "why" and they sit on a politeness ladder. なぜ is at the top — formal, composed, the kind of "why" you'd use in writing or when speaking carefully. どうして is the middle — everyday, neutral, the most common in conversation. なんで is the bottom — casual, sometimes plaintive or frustrated.

なぜですか (why is that?) sounds measured and intellectual. どうして？ sounds like a genuine question. なんで！？ sounds like you're upset. Choose wrong and the emotional temperature of your sentence shifts dramatically.

なぜ comes from 何なぜ — 何なに (what) + ぜ (an archaic particle expressing reason). "What reason?" compressed into two syllables. You'll see it written in kanji occasionally in formal texts, but hiragana is standard.

The kanji 並なみ shows two people standing side by side — two identical figures, shoulder to shoulder. That visual is the word: things arranged in a line, in a row, in parallel. 人ひとが並ならんでいる (people are lined up). 本ほんが並ならんでいる (books are lined up on a shelf).

並ならぶ is intransitive — things line themselves up. Its transitive partner 並ならべる (ならべる) means you arrange things in a row. 子供こどもが並ならんだ (the kids lined up) vs 子供こどもを並ならべた (I lined the kids up).

In Japan, queuing is an art form. 並ならぶ is what you do at ramen shops, theme parks, train platforms. 並ならんでください (please line up) is a phrase you'll hear from staff everywhere. The Japanese queue is orderly by default — the word 並ならぶ carries that expectation of neatness built in.

Particles: 並ならぶ takes に for where you line up: 列れつに並ならぶ (line up in a row). Also が for what's lined up: 店みせが並ならんでいる (shops are lined up along the street).

Passive に並ならばれる: rare for this intransitive verb. In figurative use: 彼かれに並ならばれた (he drew level with me — matched my level, which I didn't want).

Causative を／に並ならばせる: making someone line up. を for coercive: 生う徒を並ならばせる (make the students line up).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 並べる (ならべる)
Dictionaryならぶに
Negativeならばないに
Politeならびますに
Polite negならびませんに
Pastならんだに
Polite pastならびましたに
て-formならんでに
Volitionalならぼうに
Polite volならびましょうに
Passiveならばれるに
Causativeならばせるを／に
Caus-passならばせられるに
Potentialならべるに
Imperativeならべに
Imp なさいならびなさいに
Cond ～ばならべばに
Cond ～たらならんだらに

運うん has the road radical (辻つじ) with 軍ぐん (army, military) inside. An army on the move, carrying supplies along the road. That's the core image: physically transporting something from one place to another. 荷物にもつを運はこぶ (carry luggage). 料理りょうりを運はこぶ (serve food — carry dishes to the table).

The figurative extension is elegant: 足あしを運はこぶ literally means "carry your feet" — to make the trip, to go somewhere. 何なに度も足あしを運はこんだ (I went there many times — I carried my feet there repeatedly). It implies deliberate effort, not casual dropping by.

The same kanji appears in 運動うんどう (exercise, movement) and 運転うんてん (driving) — both involve moving things along a path. And 運はこ also means luck/fortune (運はこがいい = good luck), probably from the idea that your fate is carried along by forces you can't control.

Particles: 運はこぶ takes を — the thing you carry is the direct object. 荷物にもつを運はこぶ. Use に for the destination: 部へ屋やに運はこぶ (carry to the room).

Potential が運はこべる: が — what can be carried becomes the subject. これが一いち人ひとで運はこべる？ (can this be carried by one person?).

Passive に運はこばれる: に marks the carrier. 病院びょういんに運はこばれた (was carried to the hospital).

Causative に…を運はこばせる: に marks who you make carry, を marks what. 後あと輩に荷物にもつを運はこばせる (make the junior carry the luggage).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryはこぶを
Negativeはこばないを
Politeはこびますを
Polite negはこびませんを
Pastはこんだを
Polite pastはこびましたを
て-formはこんでを
Volitionalはこぼうを
Polite volはこびましょうを
Passiveはこばれるに
Causativeはこばせるに／を
Caus-passはこばせられるに
Potentialはこべるが
Imperativeはこべを
Imp なさいはこびなさいを
Cond ～ばはこべばを
Cond ～たらはこんだらを

直ちょく means straight, honest, direct — and also to fix, correct, heal. The core idea: returning something to its proper state. What's broken gets straightened. What's wrong gets righted. 直なおす is the transitive form: you fix it.

直す vs 治す: Both read なおす, both mean "to fix/cure." 直なおす is for objects and abstract things — fixing a machine, correcting a mistake, redoing homework. 治なおす is for illness and injury — curing a disease, healing a wound. Broken bike? 直なおす. Broken arm? 治なおす.

Pattern unlock: 〜直なおす attached to a verb's stem means "redo": 書かき直なおす (rewrite), やり直なおす (redo), 読よみ直なおす (reread). This is enormously useful. Any verb can become a "re-" verb by adding 直なおす.

Particles: 直なおす takes を — the broken thing is the direct object. 車くるまを直なおす (fix the car).

Potential が直なおせる: が — what can be fixed becomes the subject. これが直なおせる？ (can this be fixed?).

Passive に直なおされる: に marks who does the fixing. 先生うせんせいに作つく文を直なおされた (the teacher corrected my essay).

Causative に…を直なおさせる: に marks who you make fix it, を marks what. 子供こどもに自転車くるまを直なおさせる (make the kid fix the bicycle).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 直る (なおる)
Dictionaryなおすを
Negativeなおさないを
Politeなおしますを
Polite negなおしませんを
Pastなおしたを
Polite pastなおしましたを
て-formなおしてを
Volitionalなおそうを
Polite volなおしましょうを
Passiveなおされるに
Causativeなおさせるに／を
Caus-passなおさせられるに
Potentialなおせるが
Imperativeなおせを
Imp なさいなおしなさいを
Cond ～ばなおせばを
Cond ～たらなおしたらを

反はん (reverse, anti-) + 対たい (against, facing). Reversed and facing against — opposition in its purest form. The word works as a noun (反対はんたいがある, there's opposition), a する-verb (反対はんたいする, to oppose), and an adjective (反対はんたいの意見み, an opposing opinion).

But 反対はんたい also means "opposite" in a spatial or logical sense: 反対側はんたいがわ (はんたいがわ, the opposite side). 反対はんたい方向 (the opposite direction). So when someone says 反対はんたい, context determines whether they mean "I disagree" or "the other way around."

In democratic discourse, 賃ちん成せい (さんせい, in favor) and 反対はんたい are the two sides of every vote. 賃ちん成せいですか、反対はんたいですか (are you for or against?) is a question structure worth memorizing whole.

場ば (place, scene) + 合あい (fit, match, combine). The place where circumstances combine — a situation, a case. It's the Japanese equivalent of "in the case of" or "in the event that."

This word is a conditional workhorse. 雨あめの場合あばあい (in case of rain). 必要ひつような場合あばあい (if necessary — "in the case that it's needed"). その場合あばあい (in that case). Unlike とき (when, at the time), 場合ばあい emphasizes uncertainty — it's about hypothetical scenarios, things that might or might not happen.

You'll see it everywhere in instructions, rules, and formal communication: 火ひ事ことの場合あばあいはこの出で口くちを使つかってください (in case of fire, use this exit). It's the word that turns general statements into conditional ones.

詳くわしい does double duty. It can describe information (詳くわしい説明せつめい a detailed explanation) or a person (彼かれは日にち本ほんの歴史れきしに詳しい he's knowledgeable about Japanese history). "Detailed" when modifying things, "well-informed" when describing people. Both senses orbit the same idea: thoroughness, depth of knowledge.

The particle matters when describing a person: に marks the topic they know about. コンピューターに詳しいくわしい (knowledgeable about computers). Drop the に and it shifts to describing the information itself: 詳しいくわしいこと (detailed things / the specifics).

詳しょうしくは… (for details...) is a useful transitional phrase. 詳しょうしくはウェブサイトをご覧ください (for details, please see the website). The adverb form 詳しょうしく smoothly introduces elaboration.

Dictionaryくわしい
Negativeくわしくない
Pastくわしかった
Past negくわしくなかった
Adverbくわしく
て-formくわしくて

No kanji in common use — it's almost always written in hiragana. The word covers a range from harmless childhood pranks to more serious misconduct, depending on context. 子供こどものいたずら is cute mischief — hiding someone's shoes, drawing on a wall. いたずら電話でんわ (prank call) is less cute.

The word comes from classical Japanese 徒とら (いたづら), which originally meant "pointless, useless" — doing something with no productive purpose. The evolution from "purposeless" to "mischievous" makes sense: a prank is an act with no useful goal except causing a reaction.

いたずらする is the verb form. いたずら好すき (someone who loves pranks). In Japanese schools, いたずら ranges from the kind of thing teachers sigh about to something that borders on いじめ (bullying) — the line between the two is a constant cultural negotiation.

The お prefix here isn't optional politeness — it's fused to the word. While 祝いわい exists on its own (from the verb 祝いわう, to celebrate), you'll almost always hear the お version. Taking it off sounds curt, like handing someone a birthday card and grunting "congrats" instead of "congratulations."

The kanji 祝しゅく has the altar radical 示じ (originally a picture of a stone altar with offerings dripping down it) next to 兄あに, which here represents a person kneeling with arms raised in prayer. A person at an altar, giving thanks to the gods. That's the oldest layer of 祝いわい — not a party, but a sacred rite of gratitude. The festive meaning grew out of the ritual one.

お祝いわい covers everything from a wedding gift to a passing-the-exam dinner to the red envelope of money you hand someone at New Year's. お祝しゅくいを贈る (send a celebratory gift). お祝しゅくいの言い葉 (words of congratulation). 合あ格のお祝いに食事しょくじに行いこう (let's go eat to celebrate passing). It's both the act and the gift — context sorts out which.

Written in kanji as 櫛くし — one of those characters so complicated that almost nobody writes it. The kanji packs 木き (wood), 節ふし-related elements (joints/teeth), and strokes suggesting something with many parallel tines. A perfect picture of what it is: a wooden object with teeth. But in practice, you'll see this word in hiragana.

There's a superstition around くし as a gift: don't give one. The sounds く and し can be heard as 苦にが (suffering) and 死し (death). This kind of phonetic taboo runs deep in Japanese culture — the number four (し) gets avoided in hospitals for the same reason. A comb is a perfectly normal object to own, but handing one to someone as a present is like giving a clock in Chinese culture: the sound curses the gesture.

Things spill themselves in Japanese. こぼれる is intransitive — the water spills, the rice falls off the spoon, the tears overflow. Nobody is doing it. It just happens. コーヒーがこぼれた (the coffee spilled). If you need to blame someone, reach for the transitive partner こぼす: コーヒーをこぼした (I spilled the coffee).

The word has a surprisingly poetic second life. 涙なみだがこぼれる — tears spill, tears overflow. Not crying on purpose, not weeping dramatically. Tears that escape on their own, that you couldn't hold back. 笑えみがこぼれる — a smile spills out, breaks through despite yourself. In both cases, the intransitivity is the point: these aren't things you did. They happened to you. The emotion was too full and it overflowed.

Particles: こぼれる takes が — the spilling thing is the subject, not an object. 水みずがこぼれた (the water spilled). Use から for what it spills from: コップから水みずがこぼれた (water spilled from the glass).

Passive: Rare — since the verb is already intransitive and describes something happening on its own, passivizing it is unusual. You'd more likely use こぼす (transitive) in passive: コーヒーをこぼされた (someone spilled coffee on me).

Causative を／にこぼれさせる: making something spill. を for coercive — but in practice you'd almost always just use こぼす instead.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: こぼす
Dictionaryこぼれるが
Negativeこぼれないが
Politeこぼれますが
Polite negこぼれませんが
Pastこぼれたが
Polite pastこぼれましたが
て-formこぼれてが
Volitionalこぼれようが
Polite volこぼれましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばこぼれればが
Cond ～たらこぼれたらが

伝つたえる is what you do when something needs to get from one person to another — a message, a feeling, a tradition. Not "say" (that's 言いう) and not "teach" (that's 教おしえる). 伝つたえる is about the transmission — making sure something crosses the gap between people. 気持きもちを伝つたえる (convey your feelings). 彼かれにメッセージを伝つたえてください (please pass the message to him).

The kanji 伝でん is 亻にんべん (person) plus 云, which originally meant clouds drifting. A person passing something along like clouds drifting across the sky — things that move from here to there. The same kanji gives you 伝統でんとう (でんとう, tradition — things passed down), 伝説でんせつ (でんせつ, legend — a story passed along), and 伝つた染 (でんせん, contagion — disease passing between people). The thread is always the same: something moves from source to receiver.

Its intransitive partner 伝つたわる (つたわる) flips the perspective: the message arrives rather than being sent. 気き持もちが伝つたわった (the feelings got through). You tried to 伝つたえる; did it 伝つたわる?

Particles: 伝つたえる takes を for the content being conveyed and に for the recipient. 彼かれにニュースを伝つたえる (convey the news to him).

Potential が伝つたえられる: が marks what can be conveyed. 言い葉で伝つたえられないこともある (some things can't be conveyed with words).

Passive に伝つたえられる: に marks who does the conveying. 先さき輩に伝つたえられた話はな (a story told to me by my senior). Also commonly used for traditions: 代々伝つたえられてきた (passed down through generations).

Causative に…を伝つたえさせる: に marks who you have convey the message, を marks the content. 部へ下したにニュースを伝つたえさせた (had my subordinate deliver the news).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 伝わる (つたわる)
Dictionaryつたえるを
Negativeつたえないを
Politeつたえますを
Polite negつたえませんを
Pastつたえたを
Polite pastつたえましたを
て-formつたえてを
Volitionalつたえようを
Polite volつたえましょうを
Passiveつたえられるに
Causativeつたえさせるに／を
Caus-passつたえさせられるに
Potentialつたえられるが
Imperativeつたえろを
Imp なさいつたえなさいを
Cond ～ばつたえればを
Cond ～たらつたえたらを

膝ひざ is one of those kanji that looks impossibly complicated until you break it apart: 月にくづき (flesh/body radical) on the left tells you it's a body part, and the right side is phonetic. But forget the kanji — nobody writes it by hand. Focus on the sound: ひざ. Two syllables for one of the most culturally loaded joints in the Japanese body.

In traditional Japanese life, the knee is where you live. Sitting in 正ただ座すわ (せいざ, formal kneeling) means your knees bear your full weight for the entire visit, tea ceremony, or meal. 膝ひざが痛いたい (my knees hurt) is something you'll hear from anyone who's spent an hour on a tatami floor. The phrase 膝ひざを付くつく (to kneel, literally "attach one's knees") carries weight — it implies submission, formality, or real physical effort.

There's also 膝ひざの上うえ — on one's lap. A child sitting on your lap is on your 膝ひざ. The knee and the lap are the same word because, in a seated position, your knees are your lap.

肘ひじ — flesh radical (月にくづき) on the left, 九きゅう (nine) on the right. Why nine? The elbow bends at about a 90-degree angle — 九きゅう十じゅう度 (90 degrees) starts with nine. That's probably folk etymology rather than historical fact, but it's a useful mnemonic: nine makes an angle, elbows make angles.

肘ひじをつく (prop your elbow on the table) is considered bad manners in Japan, just like in many cultures. 肘ひじ掛かけ椅い子こ (ひじかけいす, armchair — literally "elbow-resting chair") gives you the word's most common compound.

Notice how ひざ (knee) and ひじ (elbow) sound almost identical — hi-za vs hi-ji. Both start with ひ, both are two-syllable body-part words. Mix-ups are common among learners. The trick: ざ has a harder buzz (think "za" = ground, knees touch the ground), じ is softer ("ji" = above, elbows stay up).

The kanji 枕まくら is 木き (wood) plus 冘. Traditional Japanese pillows weren't the soft poufs Westerners imagine — they were hard wooden or ceramic blocks, sometimes with a small cushion on top, designed to keep your elaborate hairstyle intact while sleeping. The wooden radical isn't metaphorical. These things were literally made of wood.

If you know any classical Japanese literature, the word 枕まくら might ring a bell: 枕まくら草くさ子こ (まくらのそうし, The Pillow Book), written around the year 1000 by Sei Shonagon. It's one of the oldest works of Japanese prose — a collection of observations, lists, and complaints kept by a woman at the imperial court. The "pillow" in the title probably refers to notes kept by the bedside, jotted down before sleep.

枕まくらが変かわると眠れない (when the pillow changes, I can't sleep) — a common Japanese saying about being unable to sleep somewhere unfamiliar. The pillow stands in for the whole bed, the whole room, the whole feeling of home.

Walk through any Japanese city and you'll notice something: buildings get described before they get named. あの白しろい建物たてもの (that white building). 駅えきの前まえの建物たてもの (the building in front of the station). Japanese addresses don't always have street names the way Western ones do, so buildings become landmarks, and 建物たてもの is the word that turns them into reference points.

The compound is transparent: 建けん (to build, from 建たてる) + 物もの (thing). A built thing. It's the all-purpose word — an apartment, a skyscraper, a shed, a temple. 建物たてもの doesn't care about size or prestige. Compare ビル (from English "building"), which specifically means a multi-story commercial structure. You'd call Tokyo Tower a 建物たてもの but never a ビル. A tiny neighborhood shrine is a 建物たてもの too.

Two kanji that both mean "road" pressed together: 道みち (みち, the path, the way) and 路 (ろ, a route or road). This kind of synonym-doubling is common in Chinese-origin compounds — the redundancy adds formality. 道路どうろ is the official, infrastructure-grade word for roads. Governments build 道路どうろ. Traffic reports talk about 道路どうろ. Signs say 道みち路工事中なか (road construction in progress).

Compare: 道みち alone is warmer, more human-scale. A path through the woods, a street you walk down, the "way" of life. 通つうり (とおり) is a named street in a city. 道路どうろ is asphalt and engineering — the physical infrastructure. 道みち路が混んでいる (the road is congested) sounds natural. 道みちが混んでいる works too but feels more casual. 高たか速道みち路 (こうそくどうろ, expressway) is one of the most common compounds.

Four corners. 四しつ (four) + 角かど (corner, angle). Where two roads cross, four corners appear. It's a wonderfully literal description of an intersection — you stand at one corner and see three others.

When giving directions in Japanese, 四しつ角かど is a key landmark word. 次つぎの四つ角を右みぎに曲がってください (turn right at the next intersection). Other direction-giving words: 曲きょくがり角かど (corner where the road bends), 信号しんごう (traffic light), 橋はし (bridge). Japanese directions tend to rely on landmarks rather than street names, since many streets in Japan don't have names the way Western streets do.

The 角かど kanji is versatile: 角度かくど (かくど, angle), 三さん角かど (さんかく, triangle), 角かど (つの, horn). Corner, angle, horn — all things that stick out at a point.

曲きょくがり (a bend, from 曲がるまがる = to turn/bend) + 角かど (corner). The corner where the road bends. Unlike 四しつ角かど (a four-way intersection), 曲きょくがり角かど is where the road itself changes direction — a turn, a bend, a corner you go around.

It also works figuratively: 人ひと生うの曲がり角 (a turning point in life). The metaphor is vivid — you can't see what's around the corner. Life bends, and you follow it into the unknown.

In giving directions: あの曲きょくがり角かどを曲きょくがって (turn at that corner). This creates the pleasant redundancy of using 曲がるまがる and 曲きょくがり角かど together — bend at the bending-corner. It sounds natural in Japanese.

警けい means to warn or admonish. 察 means to inspect or observe. Together: the people who warn and watch. The compound captures both halves of policing — deterrence and surveillance.

警察けいさつを呼ぶ (call the police) — the phrase you hope you'll never need but should absolutely know. 警察けいさつ官 (けいさつかん) is the formal word for a police officer. In casual speech, people say お巡じゅんりさん (おまわりさん) — a friendly, almost affectionate term — or just 警察けいさつ to refer to the institution and its people interchangeably.

Japanese police have a distinctive institution: 交番ばんこうばん (こうばん), the neighborhood police box. These small stations dot every few blocks in urban Japan, staffed by officers who handle everything from lost umbrellas to directions. The word 交番ばんこうばん was even borrowed into English by international policing organizations as a model for community-based law enforcement.

空そら (empty, sky) + 気き (spirit, energy, air). Empty energy — the invisible stuff that fills the space around you. The literal meaning is straightforward: the air we breathe. 空そら気がきれい (the air is clean). 空そら気を入はいれる (let air in / air out a room).

But the word you really need to know is the figurative one: 空気を読む (to read the air). This is one of the most important social skills in Japanese culture. It means sensing the unspoken mood of a room — knowing what people are thinking without them saying it, adjusting your behavior accordingly. Someone who can't 空そら気を読よむ is called KY (from 空そら気読よめない, can't read the air) — one of the more damning social labels in Japan.

空気くうき in this figurative sense is what English might call "the vibe" or "the room" — but in Japanese it's more codified, more expected. Not reading the air isn't just a social misstep; it's a failure of empathy. The word carries the full weight of Japan's indirect communication culture.

From English "sports," borrowed with the plural-ish form intact but used as a singular uncountable concept. スポーツをする (to do sports / play sports) — note: する, not やる, is the standard pairing here, though やる works too in casual speech.

Japanese uses スポーツ where English might say "athletics" or "exercise" more broadly. スポーツの秋あき (autumn of sports) is a set phrase — Japan's fall season is associated with outdoor athletic activities, partly because Sports Day (スポーツの日にち, a national holiday) falls in October. The word has a healthy, wholesome connotation that the English "sports" doesn't always carry.

One common compound: スポーツ選手てせんしゅ (athlete, literally "sports player"). And スポーツカー (sports car) — loanwords cheerfully compound with other loanwords in katakana.

Borrowed from English "chance" but the meaning narrowed on arrival. In English, "chance" can mean probability, luck, risk, or opportunity. In Japanese, チャンス almost always means opportunity — a favorable opening you should seize. チャンスを逃す (miss a chance). 今いまがチャンスだ (now's the opportunity).

For "chance" in the sense of probability or randomness, Japanese uses 可能かのう性 (かのうせい) or 確かく率 (かくりつ). For luck, 運はこ (うん). チャンス is always positive — it's the window that opens, the moment to act. You wouldn't say チャンスがある to mean "there's a risk." It's pure upside.

ピンチはチャンス — a crisis is an opportunity. It's practically a slogan in Japanese motivational culture, and you'll hear it in sports commentary, business meetings, and pep talks.

From English "cleaning," but it doesn't mean general cleaning in Japanese. クリーニング specifically means dry cleaning — the professional service. クリーニングに出です (send to the dry cleaner's) is the standard phrase. The verb is 出です (to put out, to send) because you're handing your clothes over to someone else.

For regular cleaning — mopping the floor, tidying the kitchen — Japanese uses 掃そう除 (そうじ) or 片付けるつける (かたづける). For laundry you do yourself, 洗濯せんたく (せんたく). クリーニング occupies a very specific slot: professional garment care.

This loanword took a detour on its way into Japanese. Beyond the English meaning of "service" (customer service, a service you provide), サービス developed an extra meaning: something given for free, a freebie, an extra thrown in as a bonus. これはサービスです at a restaurant means "this is on the house." サービスで付つけておきます (I'll throw it in for free).

The logic of the drift makes sense: good service means giving the customer something extra. Over time, the "extra" part became the whole meaning. In a shop, サービス品 means a discounted or bonus item. サービスする (as a する-verb) can mean to give a discount or add something gratis.

The original "service" meaning exists too — サービスが良い (the service is good) works exactly like English. You just have to be aware that the same word might mean "freebie" in a different context.

Straight from English "group" with no significant meaning shift. グループに分わかれる (split into groups). グループ活動うご (group activity). Clean and predictable.

The interesting thing about グループ is what it replaces — or doesn't replace. Japanese has native words for collections of people: 仲間ま (なかま, companions/crew) implies emotional closeness. 集あつ団 (しゅうだん) is a formal "group" with connotations of organized mass behavior. 団体からだ (だんたい) is an organization or association. グループ is neutral — no warmth, no formality, no organizational implication. Just a set of people or things lumped together. That neutrality is why it's used so broadly: school groups, work groups, music groups, discussion groups.

自じ (self) + 宅たく (residence) = one's own home. This is the word news anchors use: 容疑者しゃは自宅たくじたくで逮捕された (the suspect was arrested at his home). It's formal, objective, and neutral — it refers to someone's residence without the warmth of うち or the physical structure connotation of 家いえ (いえ).

自宅たくじたくで仕事しごとをする (work from home) became ubiquitous vocabulary in recent years. The compound 在ざい宅たく (ざいたく, being at home) uses the same 宅たく and shows up in 在ざい宅たく勤務 (ざいたくきんむ, remote work).

The kanji 宅たく is the roof radical 宀うかんむり over 乇. It's the formal, somewhat bureaucratic word for a dwelling — used in addresses (お宅たく, your home — polite), delivery services, and official documents. 自宅じたく is what you write on forms. うち is what you say to friends.

家いえ (house) + 庭にわ (garden) = house and garden, the complete domestic picture. But 家庭かてい doesn't mean a building with a yard. It means the family unit and the domestic sphere they inhabit — closer to "household" or "home life" than to "house."

家いえ庭の事情 (family circumstances) is the polite, vague way Japanese explains personal problems — financial trouble, divorce, illness — without specifying what's wrong. It's a socially accepted black box. 家いえ庭科 (かていか, home economics) is the school subject where students learn cooking and sewing. 家いえ庭教おし師 (かていきょうし, private tutor — literally "home teacher").

Compared to 家族かぞく (かぞく), which means the people in the family, 家庭かてい includes the people and the domestic environment. A dysfunctional 家庭かてい doesn't just mean bad relatives — it means the whole home situation is troubled.

期き (a set time, deadline) + 間ま (interval, space between) = a defined stretch of time with a beginning and end. Not vague "time" — 期間きかん always implies boundaries. A contract has a 期間きかん. A sale runs for a 期間きかん. A visa has a 期間きかん.

期き間ま限定 (きかんげんてい, limited-time) — you'll see this everywhere in Japan. Limited-time flavors at convenience stores, seasonal menus at restaurants, special edition products. The phrase creates urgency: this exists for a 期間きかん, then it's gone. Japanese consumer culture runs on this concept.

試し用よう期間ま (しようきかん, trial period). 保証期き間ま (ほしょうきかん, warranty period). 長なが期間ま (ちょうきかん, long period). Every 期間きかん compound reinforces the same idea: time with walls around it.

Japan's year doesn't start when you think it does. The 年度ねんど — the official year for business, government, and school — runs from April 1 to March 31. Cherry blossoms mark the beginning, not the end. New employees start in April. School starts in April. Government budgets reset in April. If someone says 今年としことし度 (こんねんど, this fiscal year) in February, they mean the year that started last April.

年とし (year) + 度ど (degree, time, measure) = a measured year, a year defined by institutional purpose rather than the calendar. 2024年とし度 means the fiscal/academic year starting April 2024. 年とし度末 (ねんどまつ, end of the fiscal year — March) is crunch time in offices across Japan: budgets get spent, reports get filed, people scramble.

This word matters because so much of Japanese scheduling revolves around 年度ねんど rather than the calendar year. Knowing that April, not January, is the real starting line explains a lot about how Japan organizes itself.

経 means to pass through. 験けん means to test or verify. Together: what you gain from passing through something — experience. Not abstract knowledge but the kind of understanding that only comes from having been there and done it.

経験けいけんする is the する-verb form: 海外そとかいがい生活せいかつを経験けいけんする (experience living abroad). But 経験けいけん as a noun is even more common: 経験けいけんがある (have experience), いい経験けいけんになった (it was a good experience). That second phrase is worth memorizing — Japanese speakers reach for it constantly, especially when something was difficult but valuable.

経験けいけん shows up on every Japanese resume. 経験けいけん者優やさ遇 (けいけんしゃゆうぐう, experienced candidates preferred) is standard in job postings. 未経験けいけん (みけいけん, no experience) marks the other side. In the Japanese workplace, what you've done matters at least as much as what you've studied.

安あん (peaceful, cheap, ease) + 全ぜん (complete, whole) = completely at peace. No danger, no risk, everything intact. The word covers both the state (safety) and the quality (safe): 安全あんぜんな場所ばしょ (a safe place), 安全あんぜんを守る (protect safety).

Japan is famously obsessed with 安全あんぜん. Construction sites have 安全あんぜん第一 (あんぜんだいいち, safety first) banners everywhere. Train announcements repeat safety reminders. Food packaging lists allergens in exhaustive detail. The culture treats safety not as a baseline but as an active, ongoing project.

Pair it with its opposite: 危険きけん (きけん, danger) — the next word in this list. 安全あんぜん and 危険きけん are two sides of the same coin and frequently appear together: 安全あんぜんか危険きけんか (safe or dangerous?).

Dictionaryあんぜんだ
Negativeあんぜんじゃない
Pastあんぜんだった
Past negあんぜんじゃなかった
Adverbあんぜんに
て-formあんぜんで

危き is a person crouching on a cliff edge — the kanji itself looks precarious. 険 means steep or perilous, with the hill radical 阝こざとへん on the left. Together: clinging to a precipice. The visual is baked into the characters.

危険きけん works as both a noun and a な-adjective. 危険きけんな目めに遭う (encounter a dangerous situation). 危険きけんを冒す (take a risk). 命いのちに危険きけんがある (there is danger to life). Signs screaming 危険きけん！ appear near construction sites, electrical equipment, cliffs — anywhere injury is possible.

There's a useful shortened form: 危ないあぶない (あぶない), the い-adjective version of "dangerous." 危険きけん is the formal, official word — the one on warning labels. 危ないあぶない is what you shout when someone is about to step into traffic. Same concept, different registers.

Dictionaryきけんだ
Negativeきけんじゃない
Pastきけんだった
Past negきけんじゃなかった
Adverbきけんに
て-formきけんで

注ちゅう (to pour) + 意い (mind, intention) = to pour your mind into something. That's attention at its most visceral — not passive awareness but actively directing your mental energy somewhere.

The word does quadruple duty. As a warning: 注意ちゅうい！足あし元に気をつけて (caution! watch your step). As attention: 注意ちゅういを払はらう (pay attention). As a scolding: 先生うせんせいに注意ちゅういされた (the teacher warned/scolded me). As care: 注意ちゅうい深ふかい (careful, cautious).

That third use — being scolded — catches learners off guard. 注意ちゅういする when directed at a person doesn't mean "to pay attention to them." It means "to caution them, to call them out, to give them a talking-to." 子供こどもを注意ちゅういする = scold the child. 子供こどもに注意ちゅういする = pay attention to the child. The particle makes the difference: を makes them the target of correction, に makes them the object of your focus.

In Japanese, you don't just "achieve" success — you harvest it. 成功せいこうを収める (achieve success) uses the verb 収める, which literally means to reap or gather in. Success is a crop. You plant effort, you tend it, you wait, and eventually you harvest 成功せいこう. The agricultural metaphor is telling: this culture doesn't believe in overnight success.

成功せいこう pairs naturally with 努ど力ちから (effort) — in Japanese thinking, the two are practically inseparable. 努ど力ちからなしに成功はない (there is no success without effort) isn't just a proverb; it's a cultural assumption. 実じつ験が成功せいこうした (the experiment succeeded). 大おお成功 (たいせいこう, great success).

Its opposite is 失敗 (しっぱい, failure), and the two constantly orbit each other: 成功せいこうも失敗も経験けいけんだ (both success and failure are experience). The kanji themselves tell the story: 成せい (to accomplish) + 功 (merit) — achievement through earned merit, not luck.

If there's one word that captures the Japanese work ethic in two syllables, it might be 努力どりょく. 努ど means to exert oneself (it contains 力ちから, power, at the bottom — power being squeezed out). 力ちから is force. Together: the application of sustained force toward a goal.

Japanese culture values 努力どりょく as a near-universal explanation for outcomes. Talent matters, but effort is what gets respect. 努ど力ちからすれば必ずできる (if you put in the effort, you can definitely do it) is the kind of thing teachers, parents, and coaches say constantly. Whether or not it's always true, the belief in 努力どりょく as the path to success is foundational.

努ど力ちから家いえ (どりょくか, a hard worker — a person defined by their effort) is always a compliment. もっと努ど力ちからしなさい (try harder) is what you hear when you're falling short. The word carries both encouragement and pressure in equal measure.

Buy any appliance in Japan and it comes with a 説明あかせつめい書か (せつめいしょ, instruction manual) that could double as a novella. Pages of diagrams, warnings, edge cases. That thoroughness is 説明せつめい in its natural habitat — the Japanese instinct to explain completely, leaving no gap for misunderstanding.

説明あかせつめいする is one of the most-used する-verbs in classrooms, meetings, and customer service. もう一いち度説明あかせつめいしてください (please explain one more time). The kanji paint a picture of what good explanation does: 説 (to explain) + 明あか (bright, clear) — words that bring light into a dark room.

Watch the grammar closely: Xについて（人ひと）に説明あかせつめいする — the person you explain to takes に, the topic takes について or を. 学がく生うにルールを説明あかせつめいする (explain the rules to the students). Getting these particles right is the difference between being understood and causing the very confusion you were trying to clear up.

地ち (ground, earth) + 震しん (to shake, tremble). The ground shakes. No metaphor, no abstraction — the word is a statement of physical fact.

If you live in or visit Japan, this is survival vocabulary. Japan experiences thousands of earthquakes per year, most too small to notice, some devastating. 地震しんじしんが来くた (an earthquake came) — note the verb: earthquakes come in Japanese, like a visitor arriving. 大おお地震 (だいじしん or おおじしん, a big earthquake). 震しん度ど (しんど, seismic intensity) is the Japanese scale you'll hear on every earthquake broadcast — it measures how much the ground shakes at a specific location, from 1 (barely felt) to 7 (catastrophic).

The kanji 震しん is 雨あめ (rain) over 辰 (dragon). In ancient Chinese mythology, dragons caused storms and earthquakes — heavenly beasts whose movements made the earth tremble. The character preserves that old belief: rain and dragons, the powers that shake the world.

手て (hand) + 術じゅつ (technique, art). Surgery as a hand-art — the technique of the hands. 術じゅつ is the same kanji in 技術じゅつ (ぎじゅつ, technology/skill) and 芸術じゅつげいじゅつ (げいじゅつ, art). It always implies mastery of a craft.

手て術を受ける (to undergo surgery — literally "receive" it) is the standard patient-side phrase. 手て術する is what the doctor does. 手て術室 (しゅじゅつしつ, operating room). 手て術が成功した (the surgery was successful).

Pronunciation note: しゅじゅつ is a tongue-twister for many learners. The じゅ in the middle gets swallowed easily. Practice it slowly: shu-ju-tsu. In fast speech, native speakers sometimes blur it to something like しじつ, but you should aim for the full form.

The kanji tell you everything: 火ひ (fire) + 傷きず (wound, injury). A fire-wound. A burn. But the reading やけど comes from the native Japanese verb 焼けるやける (やける, to burn/be burned) — やけど is the noun form of what happens when you get burned.

火ひ傷きずをする (to get burned) — note that you "do" a burn to yourself, using する. 熱あついお茶ちゃで火傷した (I burned myself with hot tea). It covers everything from a minor kitchen scalding to a serious injury.

There's an idiom worth knowing: 大おお火傷をする in a figurative sense means to suffer a major setback — to "get burned" in business, a relationship, or a risky venture. Same metaphor as English, same visceral punch.

課か (lesson, section, to assign) + 題だい (topic, title, problem). An assigned topic — something handed to you that needs solving or completing. In school: homework, a class project, a research assignment. In the workplace: a pending task, an unsolved problem, an area for improvement.

The workplace sense is the one that gives 課題かだい its real weight. 今いま後あとの課題 (challenges going forward) is a phrase you'll hear at the end of every Japanese business presentation. It means "things we still need to figure out" — a formal way of acknowledging that the work isn't done. 課か題だいを解決きする (solve a problem/task). 大おおきな課題が残のこっている (a major challenge remains).

Compare with 宿題だいしゅくだい (しゅくだい, homework), which is specifically schoolwork assigned to take home. 課題かだい is broader — any assigned challenge, in any context.

You already know 子供こども (こども, child). 子こ by itself is shorter, warmer, and more versatile. いい子こ (a good kid). あの子こ (that kid/that girl). 男おとこの子こ / 女おんなの子こ (boy/girl). It's affectionate — calling someone 子こ implies smallness, youth, or endearment.

But 子こ's real power is as a suffix. It attaches to words to create names for living things, concepts, or even people. 子こ猫ねこ (こねこ, kitten). 子こ犬いぬ (こいぬ, puppy). The 子こ means "young one." In personal names, it's the most common ending for women's names — 花はな子こ, 洋子こ, 京子こ — though this trend peaked mid-century and is now less dominant among younger generations.

The kanji itself is one of the simplest: a picture of a baby with outstretched arms. The horizontal stroke is the arms, the vertical stroke is the body, the bottom hook is the swaddling cloth. It's been a baby for 3,000 years.

確かく (certain, sure) + 認 (to recognize, acknowledge). To make something certain by recognizing it — verification. This is the word of Japanese conscientiousness.

You will hear 確認かくにんしてください (please confirm/verify) in every professional setting: offices, airports, hospitals, restaurants. Japanese work culture runs on double-checking. Before the train departs, the conductor does 指差し確認かくにん (しさしかくにん, point-and-confirm) — physically pointing at each signal and gauge while calling out its status. This system, invented by Japanese railways, has been adopted worldwide because it dramatically reduces human error.

予約よやくの確認かくにん (reservation confirmation). もう一いち度確認かくにんする (check one more time). 確認かくにんが取とれた (confirmation has been obtained). The word is everywhere because the habit of verifying is everywhere.

実じつ (fruit, truth, reality) + 際さい (occasion, boundary, actual). Reality at the boundary — what you find when you get close enough to see what's really there, past the assumptions.

実際じっさい is the pivot word that flips expectations. Whatever came before it was theory, rumor, or assumption. Whatever comes after is what's actually true. 簡単かんたんそうだけど、実際は難しい (it looks easy, but in reality it's hard). 実際じっさいに会あってみたら、いい人ひとだった (when I actually met them, they were nice).

It works both as a noun (実際じっさいのところ, in actuality) and as an adverb (実際じっさいに, actually, in practice). The に form is more common in conversation. You'll reach for this word whenever you need to contrast what people think with what's real — which, in any language, is surprisingly often.

In the 1980s, Japan was gripped by a buzzword: 国くに際化 (こくさいか, internationalization). The country was becoming an economic superpower and needed to engage with the world. Politicians said it. Businesspeople said it. Schools launched English programs in its name. Whether the project succeeded is still debated, but the word 国際こくさい embedded itself permanently in the vocabulary.

国際こくさい works like a prefix — it doesn't stand alone the way English "international" does. It bolts onto nouns: 国くに際空港くうこう (international airport), 国くに際電話はなでんわ (international phone call), 国くに際関係 (international relations). To make it a full adjective, add 的てき: 国くに際的な問題もんだい (an international issue).

The kanji: 国くに (country) + 際さい (boundary, between). The same 際さい from 実際じっさい — there it means "at the edge of reality," here it means the space where countries meet. Borders are where the interesting things happen.

会あ (to meet) + 議ぎ (to discuss, deliberate). People meeting to discuss things. That's a meeting. The compound is formal and workplace-specific — you wouldn't use 会議かいぎ for getting together with friends.

会あ議中なか (かいぎちゅう, in a meeting) is the Japanese equivalent of a closed door. It means "do not disturb." 会あ議に出でる (attend a meeting). 会あ議を開あく (hold a meeting — literally "open" one). 会あ議室 (かいぎしつ, meeting room/conference room).

Japanese meetings have a reputation for being long and consensus-driven. The concept of 根こん回かいし (ねまわし, behind-the-scenes groundwork) means that by the time the formal 会議かいぎ happens, the real decisions have often already been made through informal conversations. The meeting itself is where the agreement gets formalized and everyone nods together. Understanding this helps you understand why 会議かいぎ in Japan can feel ritualistic rather than deliberative.

After all the 根こん回かいし (behind-the-scenes groundwork) is done, someone still has to formally put the idea on the table. That's 提案ていあん. 会あ議で新あたらしいプランを提案した (I proposed a new plan at the meeting). いい提てい案あんがある (I have a good suggestion).

It sits in a middle register: more formal than アイデア (a casual idea you toss out) but less weighty than 提てい言い (ていげん, a formal recommendation from an expert body). 提案ていあん is what you do in a meeting — you present a worked-out suggestion for others to consider.

The kanji 案あん has a lovely image buried in it: 木き (tree) over 安あん (peace). Sitting peacefully under a tree — the place where ideas come to you. Newton had his apple tree; Japanese has it baked into the character for "plan." The same 案あん appears in 案あん内うち (あんない, guidance) and 答こた案 (とうあん, answer sheet).

事こと務 (business affairs, clerical work) + 所しょ (place). The place where business gets done. Not a corner office in a skyscraper — 事こと務所 is more modest than that. A law office. A talent agency. A local government office. The word implies a working space where paperwork and administrative tasks happen.

事こと務所に戻もどる (return to the office). 弁護士し事こと務所 (べんごしじむしょ, law office). 芸げい能事こと務所 (げいのうじむしょ, talent agency) — in the Japanese entertainment industry, 事こと務所 is the standard word for the agency that manages celebrities.

Compare: オフィス (from English "office") tends to refer to modern corporate workspaces, especially in tech and international business. 事こと務所 is broader and more traditional — the default Japanese word for any working office.

教おし (to teach) + 授じゅ (to give, bestow, confer). Teaching as a bestowal — not just passing along information but conferring knowledge with authority. The word carries a weight that the casual English "professor" sometimes doesn't.

In Japanese academia, 教授きょうじゅ is a specific rank — the highest regular faculty position, above 准教おし授 (じゅんきょうじゅ, associate professor) and 助じょ教おし (じょきょう, assistant professor). You don't call every university teacher 教授きょうじゅ — that would be like calling every doctor a surgeon. When addressing a professor, the title comes after the name: 田た中なか教おし授. Or simply 教おし授 by itself, the way English speakers might say "Professor."

As a する-verb, 教おし授する means "to teach" in a formal, somewhat archaic sense — to instruct with expertise. You're more likely to see this in writing than to hear it in conversation.

世せ (generation, world, era) + 紀き (chronicle, era, record). A recorded era — a century. 21世せ紀 (にじゅういっせいき, the 21st century). Simple, clean, works the same way as English.

But 世紀せいき has a dramatic second life in the phrase 世せ紀の — "of the century," meaning once-in-a-lifetime, era-defining. 世せ紀の大おお発見み (discovery of the century). 世せ紀の大おお事件 (incident of the century). Japanese media loves this construction. Anything sufficiently dramatic gets the 世せ紀の stamp.

The kanji 紀き contains the thread radical 糸 — the idea of spinning out a thread of time, weaving the chronicle. 世せ originally depicted three sets of ten (三十), meaning a generation — roughly thirty years. Three characters that tell the story of time passing and being recorded.
